architecture.summary: Verifiable Process Reward Models (VPRMs) integrate deterministic, rule-based verifiers into reinforcement learning to evaluate intermediate reasoning steps in structured tasks. The model generates a reasoning trajectory with step identifiers and step labels; each step is checked against domain guidelines by a deterministic verifier (not a neural judge), yielding step-level rewards. These step rewards are combined with terminal outcome rewards and fed into policy optimization algorithms (GRPO or DAPO) to guide the LLM toward reasoning traces that are both factually correct and logically coherent with task rules.
trained_components: reasoning trajectory generation with step labels

biblio.title: Beyond Outcome Verification: Verifiable Process Reward Models for Structured Reasoning

claims.statement: VPRMs achieve up to 20% higher F1 than state-of-the-art models
VPRMs achieve 6.5% higher F1 than verifiable outcome rewards
On CochraneForest, Qwen2.5-7B-GRPO-VPRM achieves 87.9% Accuracy and 76.7% macro-F1
VPRM-trained models achieve 89.5% Coherence on CochraneForest (proportion of datapoints where final risk prediction is consistent with intermediate reasoning)
VPRM-trained models (Qwen2.5-7B-GRPO-VPRM) achieve 75.0% Coherent Accuracy (accuracy restricted to coherent instances)
Neural process-reward baselines (Qwen2.5-7B-GRPO-PRM-GPT-OSS) achieve 78.2% Accuracy and 56.1% F1 on CochraneForest, consistently outperformed by VPRM

Under mild reward-separation assumptions (finite variance, correct reasoning receives strictly larger expected reward than incorrect, sufficiently large group size), verifiable process rewards offer theoretical guarantees that gradient-based updates assign positive expected weight to correct reasoning trajectories and negative weight to inconsistent ones

VPRMs rely on the existence of deterministic, domain-specific rules; tasks lacking well-defined intermediate reasoning steps may not benefit directly. Empirical evaluation is currently focused on risk-of-bias assessment; generalisation to other domains, particularly open-ended reasoning tasks, remains to be established.
